concepts.lesson: The poem describes a quiet, invisible man who is responsible for every mishap in the house. He is a humorous personification of the mistakes we don't want to admit to.
concepts.real_example: When a plate is found cracked and no one knows how it happened, it is attributed to the 'funny man'.
key_points: He is as quiet as a mouse
He does all the mischief in the house

concepts.lesson: The poem highlights how every household experiences the same small problems. It connects readers through shared, everyday experiences.
concepts.real_example: Finding muddy footprints on the carpet or leaving a door ajar.
key_points: Common accidents happen in every home
Small oversights are part of life

concepts.lesson: The 'Funny Man' represents the gap between an action and taking responsibility. It explores why it is often easier to blame an invisible force than ourselves.
concepts.real_example: A person choosing to stay silent when a window is broken rather than admitting fault.
key_points: The convenience of an anonymous excuse
Human nature and the fear of trouble
The difference between intent and accident

concepts.lesson: The poet uses specific images like scattered pins, unbuttoned shirts, and damp wood to show how small acts of neglect create a messy environment.
concepts.real_example: Misplacing the 'papers' or letting the 'fire' go out because of forgetfulness.
key_points: Small actions lead to visible clutter
Descriptive language creates a mental picture
The contrast between order and mischief

concepts.lesson: The poem is ironic because it claims that 'Mr. Nobody' breaks the plates, yet we all know it is actually the people living in the house.
concepts.real_example: Everyone agreeing that they 'never see his face,' yet everyone 'agrees' he is the one to blame.
key_points: The joke shared by all family members

concepts.lesson: By identifying 'Mr. Nobody,' the poem encourages us to be more mindful of our actions and perhaps more courageous in admitting our small errors.
concepts.real_example: Realizing that 'I' was the one who left the door squeaking, not a 'funny man'.
